baseline_dominance.justification: The construction identifies a genuine capability gap (proving multi-hop narrowing without disclosing intermediate scopes or participants, without an AS). However, the rfc7662_advocate's Attack 4 correctly notes that an AS can already provide offline narrowing attestations via signed JWT introspection responses without disclosing intermediate scopes to the auditor — the 'cannot' in §8 is overstated. The real differentiator is narrower: AS-free cross-org narrowing proof where no single party holds plaintext scopes. Additionally, the spiffe_engineer's Attack 3 and rfc7662_advocate's Attack 1 show that allDelegateesMerkleRoot is effectively a shared trust anchor (just on-chain instead of HTTPS), weakening the 'no trusted third party' claim. The baseline can approximate with a trusted AS issuing signed narrowing attestations; the construction's advantage is real but overclaimed.

formal_security_argument.justification: The construction has explicit threat model, three game definitions (GAME-NARROW, GAME-HIDE, GAME-FORGE), named cryptographic assumptions (DL-BJJ, CR-Poseidon, KS-Groth16, ZK-Groth16), and reduction sketches. However, the cryptographer's attacks expose critical gaps: (1) GAME-NARROW does not cover chain fidelity — colluding agents can fabricate shadow chains that pass all checks (Attack 1, history rewriting); (2) currentTimestamp is prover-controlled and not bound into auditDigest, making expiry liveness vacuous (Attack 2); (3) GAME-HIDE's precondition requires identical public outputs which real deployments won't satisfy (Attack 3); (4) PLONK SRS ceremony provenance is unspecified (Attack 4). The spiffe_engineer's Attack 2 adds that active[h] is prover-controlled, meaning chainLength is asserted not proven. Multiple game definitions are incomplete or inapplicable to real deployment conditions.

implementability.justification: The construction maps directly to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon2/4, EdDSAPoseidonVerifier, BinaryMerkleRoot(20), Num2Bits(64), LessThan/LessEqThan — all from circomlib/@zk-kit already used in the Delegation circuit. Circuit cost is explicitly estimated at ~139,400 constraints for 8-hop (fits pot18.ptau), ~69,700 for 4-hop (fits pot16.ptau). Proving time targets stated (<5s for 8-hop PLONK). The construction composes the same per-hop constraints as the existing Delegation circuit iteratively. PLONK choice justified for agent-class (no human secret). Verification cost ~300K gas. All within Phase 1 budget.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 adversaries found multiple attacks the construction cannot answer without significant redesign: (1) cryptographer's shadow chain attack (GAME-NARROW doesn't cover chain fidelity — no on-chain binding of actual delegation tokens used at runtime); (2) prover-controlled currentTimestamp with no freshness enforcement or auditDigest binding; (3) GAME-HIDE precondition too strong for real cross-org deployments; (4) spiffe_engineer's active[h] attack — chainLength is prover-asserted, not cryptographically enforced; (5) auth0_pm's prover assembly problem — no specified protocol for aggregating private witness across adversarially-separated hops (especially fatal for whistleblower scenario). These are not mitigated attacks; they are unanswered structural flaws.

scenario_fit.justification: The Navy Federal scenario is concrete and names a real stakeholder (NFCU, NCUA examiner, GENIUS Act). However, the cu_ciso's attacks reveal the scenario is poorly fitted: (1) the proof covers delegation integrity but not behavioral integrity — the examiner's actual question ('which agent authorized the $47K transfer to a sanctioned wallet?') cannot be answered; (2) the proof artifact is not examiner-legible (no FFIEC mapping, no SOC 2, no control framework alignment); (3) witness custody creates a GLBA data retention problem unaddressed by the construction; (4) the auditor cannot verify the proof without BN128-aware tooling. The scenario is named but the construction doesn't actually solve the examiner's load-bearing requirement — it solves a subset (delegation narrowing) that the examiner needs wrapped in a regulatory attestation layer not specified.

gaps: Add GAME-FAITHFUL: per-hop delegation nullifiers must be public outputs checked against on-chain nullifier registry to prevent shadow-chain forgery
Bind currentTimestamp into auditDigest (Poseidon5) and specify on-chain freshness check (currentTimestamp >= block.timestamp - STALENESS_WINDOW)
Specify witness aggregation protocol for multi-party proof generation — recursive proving or MPC witness assembly for adversarially-separated hops
Replace single allDelegateesMerkleRoot with per-hop root array or set-membership proof against a roots commitment to support true cross-org without shared registry
Make chainLength cryptographically enforced via on-chain nullifier chain completeness check, not prover-asserted via active[h]
Narrow §8 baseline comparison: 'cannot without trusting an AS' not 'cannot' — acknowledge AS-issued signed attestations as a valid approximation
Add scope-blinding salt to Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credCommitment) so identical scopes don't produce fingerprintable commitments
Address regulatory legibility: specify attestation wrapper layer that maps proof semantics to FFIEC/Part 748 controls for examiner consumption
Scope deployment claim to 'delegation integrity audit' explicitly, not behavioral audit — or add per-hop action log commitments
